verses.text: The beloved enters his garden, gathers his myrrh and honey, and bids his friends, 'eat, O friends; drink abundantly.' He delights in his bride and shares his joy. Christ rejoices over His people and welcomes others to the feast of His love.

She sleeps, yet her heart wakes to his knock: 'Open to me'; but she has put off her coat and hesitates. Drowsy love delays. The soul, grown careless, is slow to answer Christ's call and so loses the sweetness of His nearness.

She sleeps, yet her heart wakes to his knock: 'Open to me'; but she has put off her coat and hesitates. Drowsy love delays. The soul, grown careless, is slow to answer Christ's call and so loses the sweetness of His nearness.

She sleeps, yet her heart wakes to his knock: 'Open to me'; but she has put off her coat and hesitates. Drowsy love delays. The soul, grown careless, is slow to answer Christ's call and so loses the sweetness of His nearness.
Rising at last to open, she finds her beloved withdrawn; she seeks but cannot find him. Delay has cost her his presence. When we put off communion with Christ, we may find His felt nearness gone.
Rising at last to open, she finds her beloved withdrawn; she seeks but cannot find him. Delay has cost her his presence. When we put off communion with Christ, we may find His felt nearness gone.

The watchmen smite and wound her; she charges others, if they find her beloved, to tell him she is 'sick of love.' Her longing is now intense. The grieved soul seeks Christ through every hardship, sick with desire for Him.

The watchmen smite and wound her; she charges others, if they find her beloved, to tell him she is 'sick of love.' Her longing is now intense. The grieved soul seeks Christ through every hardship, sick with desire for Him.

Asked what makes her beloved special, she answers, 'My beloved is white and ruddy, the chiefest among ten thousand.' He is beyond compare. To the loving heart Christ outshines all rivals; none can match Him.

Asked what makes her beloved special, she answers, 'My beloved is white and ruddy, the chiefest among ten thousand.' He is beyond compare. To the loving heart Christ outshines all rivals; none can match Him.

Asked what makes her beloved special, she answers, 'My beloved is white and ruddy, the chiefest among ten thousand.' He is beyond compare. To the loving heart Christ outshines all rivals; none can match Him.

She describes his eyes, cheeks, hands, and countenance, ending, 'his mouth is most sweet: yea, he is altogether lovely.' Every part of him delights her. The believer finds Christ wholly and supremely lovely, the friend above all friends.
